title: Finding the Question I've Been Asking All Along
body:
In June 2023 I sat down with four years of coursework, fieldwork notes, and informal conversations with friends about music, and I saw the same question running through all of it. In Abu Dhabi and Ethiopia I was asking how cultural context shapes the way people connect. In Ben Ratliff's seminar I was mapping my own listening patterns backward through my life. In the rominimal paper I was tracing a minimalist thread across a century of Romanian art. In the listening sessions I had been running informally, I was sitting with friends and trying to understand why certain music lands the way it does. The question in every case was the same: how does where you come from shape how you hear?

The answer I kept arriving at: you carry your listening forward along grooves carved by your origins, your childhood, the sonic world that surrounded you before you ever made a conscious choice. Those patterns persist across decades and genres. From that realisation came a name and a concept. Time Compass is a tool that takes your listening history, Spotify exports, rated tracks, anything timestamped, and finds the temporal patterns in it. What shaped your taste at 15, at 22, at the moments your listening shifted? The system generates a map from your genesis point outward, surfaces music that belongs to your thread, and gives you language for who you are as a listener. The concept is solid. The technical shape is still forming.

title: How We Inherit the Way We Listen
body:
My interest in minimal electronic music had always felt personal, a taste I couldn't fully explain. Then I read Judith Becker's work on listening habitus in Ben Ratliff's seminar at NYU and found the explanation I was looking for. Becker showed that listening is absorbed like an accent, taken in unconsciously from the people and places around you. You carry your listening forward without realising it. When I mapped my own patterns backward through my life, I found something underneath personal preference: a thread running from where I came from.

That thread became concrete when I wrote my Music Histories paper on rominimal. Tracing the Romanian minimal aesthetic across a century, from Brancusi's sculpture to George Enescu's violin compositions to jazz musician Johnny Raducanu to the electronic producers of today, I kept finding the same reductionist impulse: amplification of effect through reduction of means. Each generation inherited it and applied it to whatever sounds were available to them. The theorist Eyerman and Jamison call this mobilizing tradition, connecting a usable past with the present. Rominimal was proof that listening cultures have memory, and that memory passes forward across generations and mediums. If it worked for a whole country, it worked for a person too. Every listener carries threads from their own origin point. That realisation became the foundation of Time Compass.

title: How Context, Why Context
body:
My journey into studying listening started in a psychology course at NYU Abu Dhabi. Professor Niobe Way took our class to Addis Ababa, Ethiopia for a week of fieldwork, where we interviewed students at the Medhen Social Center. For my final paper, I ended up studying something I lived myself: how multilingual people associate different languages with different emotions. I am bilingual in English and Malayalam, and I had always felt the pull to switch languages when my feelings changed. I wanted to understand why.

What I found was that the emotional meaning of a language comes from your origin point, your home environment, your school, your cultural context. A 17-year-old I interviewed, Y, associated his childhood language Sudaminya with trust and loss after leaving his village at age 8. A 14-year-old, M, told me she speaks her mother's language Guaringya when she is happy, because her mother is the reason she learned it at all. She also said something that stayed with me: before she learned Guaringya, her grandmother could only tell her shortened versions of old stories through translation. Once she learned the language herself, she could finally draw the full picture in her mind.

That image became the seed of Time Compass. If the full story requires the original language, then the full listening experience requires access to your own origin. The way you hear music is shaped by everything that formed you before you ever chose a song. I wanted to build something that could help people trace that thread.

title: Finding the Sweet Spot: How Rhythm Training Might Rewire Your Nervous System
body:
I've had a major breakthrough in how I'm measuring what happens when we practice rhythmic stick work. It turns out that the tiny, invisible gaps between heartbeats tell a story about whether your nervous system is healthy and adaptable—or stressed and rigid. 

I switched to tracking something called DFA alpha, which reads the fractal structure of your heartbeat (Heart rate variability, to be precise). Think of it like this: a truly healthy heart doesn't beat like a metronome. Instead, it wiggles in this sweet, flexible way—the kind of adaptability that lets you respond to life's surprises. That sweet spot lands around 1.0 on the DFA alpha scale. Drop toward 0.5 and things look chaotic; climb toward 1.5 and you get rigidity.

Here's where it clicked: my hypothesis is that rhythmic practice—the ⦿-⦿—trains your nervous system toward exactly that adaptive state. 

I'm now collaborating with Dmitry and his team at 8OS on an app called SomaSync that measures DFA alpha in real time using an Apple Watch or chest strap. We're planning two tiers: a quick proof of concept anyone can try now, then a more rigorous study with better equipment and a bigger group.

The intellectual thread finally tied together. It's about keeping time—and it's about building the nervous system's capacity to be both organized and flexible.

title: Theory to Action: A Smarter Study Design
body:
When I started this project, I was planning a five-week study with washout periods and multiple heart-rate sensors—the kind of setup that looked good on paper but would've been logistically messy and hard to repeat across different groups. Over the past couple of months, that changed entirely.

The breakthrough came from rethinking the problem from the ground up. Instead of spreading measurements across weeks, I'm now capturing everything within a single ~15-minute session. Here's how: participants do three short blocks—a resting baseline, then two conditions (one with the stick, one without) while playing a biofeedback "game" that advances only when they hit the right heart-rate zone. It's clean, it controls for daily variation, and it gives me a real behavioral measure: how quickly they can access that zone.

The other big shift was going from multiple sensors to *one* rotating heart-rate monitor across participants. This sounds like a constraint, but it's actually freed things up—I can now test many more people across different real-world settings in the same timeframe, which makes the results more meaningful.I built the whole system from scratch (JavaScript, Web Bluetooth, live heart-rate analysis) and locked in the final design last month.

Now we're ready. Pilot testing starts June 1st with five people to estimate our effect size, then I'll use that to calculate exactly how many participants I need. It's the point where planning stops and real fieldwork begins.

title: Five Weeks, One Control, Many Open Questions
body:
After months of iterating, I've finally locked down the core design: a five-week study where half the participants practice rhythmic movement with a metronome for the first two weeks, then rest, then do a control activity — and the other half reverses the order. I'll track heart rate variability throughout using a wearable, plus daily five-minute sessions and questionnaires before and after each phase.

The big question I'm testing is whether practicing this way actually creates more stable, regular movement patterns over time. There are some secondary questions too (like whether heart rate improves), but the design is now clear enough to run.

What surprised me most during this push was surfacing all the things I *don't* know yet. like whether the control condition should be sitting still, moving without rhythm, or something else entirely. Each choice tells a different story about what the practice actually does. I also flagged that a one-week break between phases might not be long enough for the body to fully "reset," which could muddy the results.

On a personal note, my main supervisor took a professorship at Medical School Berlin, so Dr. B is stepping in as primary supervisor. It's a smooth transition, and it's also given me clear feedback: the proposal had gotten too complicated. Time to simplify, nail down the details that matter, and get moving.

title: Lab to Life: Why I'm Taking My Thesis Outside
body:
When I presented my original plan at the colloquium in January, the faculty gently redirected me. My designed lab experiment was sound, but they asked: *Can you actually build this? Will it get ethical approval? And who will it help?* That question changed everything.

I'd been focused on proving a quick, controlled effect. Instead, I've pivoted to something bigger: understanding whether coordinating breath and movement is a *learnable skill* that people can develop over weeks and months of real practice — outside a lab, in their actual lives.

The shift unlocked a new approach. Rather than measuring acute responses, I'm now tracking how people's heart-rate variability (a marker of nervous system flexibility) evolves as they practice. My collaborators and I built Loob, a simple motion tracker that works with just a webcam, and we're partnering with 8OS, a peer project whose HRV software aligns perfectly with the physiology I'm investigating.


I'm moving to running two tiers: a proof-of-concept starting this month with just Apple Watches, and a larger study if funding comes through. For the first time, this feels like something that could actually work — and matter.

title: From Brain to Hearts: How my Thesis Found Its Rhythm
body:
When I (re)started this journey in July 2025, I was deep in neuroscience papers trying to build a theory about how a particular brain receptor might explain altered states of consciousness. It felt ambitious and distant from anything I could actually test—and I was running out of time. My visa expires at the end of September, and my degree depends on it.

Then something unexpected happened. I found a way to integrate my underground research into my thesis work in cognitive neuroscience: the ⦿-⦿. It's simple, tactile, and there's already a community of about 33 people using it informally. That opened a door.

My focus shifted from abstract brain chemistry to something I could measure: what happens to people's hearts when they move with rhythm? Specifically, I'm now asking whether spinning the ⦿-⦿ in time with music can synchronize someone's heart rate and breathing in measurable ways. It sounds niche, but it touches on something real—how our bodies entrain to external rhythms.

The design began coming into focus: I'll test whether the stick helps people sync their physiology to music, whether it matters if they're moving *with* or *against* their natural rhythm, and whether I can capture these changes using affordable heart-rate sensors.

I have a willing participant pool right here in Berlin. The hypothesis object has shifted from emotion to physiology, from theory to data. And honestly? That feels like progress.

title: From Fidgeting to Breathing: How a Spinning Stick Became a Window into Physiology
body:
When I started this project, I was looking into e-motions; trying to understand how rhythmic fidgeting might calm us down. But somewhere along the way, my research found its heartbeat.

The turning point came when I discovered **locomotor–respiratory coupling (LRC)**: the scientific principle that rhythmic movement and breathing naturally synchronise. It's well-documented for walking and cycling, but nobody had tested it with something simple—like spinning a stick. That became my question: can a gentle, rhythmic movement entrain your breathing without needing to work hard physically?

This reframe felt like finding the missing puzzle piece. Instead of measuring emotions (which are slippery), I'm now measuring heart-rate variability and respiratory patterns during and after spinning—clean, quantifiable signals that reveal what's actually happening in your body.

My design crystallised: a controlled lab study with about ten people, each doing four conditions (spinning with sound, spinning in silence, sitting still with sound, sitting still in silence). I'm tracking physiological recovery—how well their nervous system settles afterwards.

The real test comes in January. I'm presenting this at a research colloquium, then launching the first experiments by spring. What excites me most isn't the fidgeting anymore,it's proving that something so simple can genuinely shift how our bodies work.
